subject: Support truststore with AAF root certs
commit_message:
Support truststore with AAF root certs

Change-Id: Ib7f2e9ee556dd040b097e6f47453de6e231a56c8
Issue-ID: POLICY-747
Signed-off-by: Jorge Hernandez <jh1730@att.com>

entries.message:
Patch Set 2:

Jorge, should they switch to the SNAPSHOT image for this to work?

Patch Set 2:

This change should not cause any problems with standing up  policy with helm, and should be OK to pass the healthcheck.   It will only come into play when using control loops, querying AAI, etc .., in that case, yes the image should 1.2.1-SNAPHOT is needed.  But we haven't reached that point yet to verify use cases with OOM set up.

Patch Set 2:

Mike, the errors you are showing have nothing to do with these changes.  These changes do not affect the AAF container.  This change only affect the policy helm charts.  The change is fairly trivial and just replaces a cert store with another one.   Just to make it clear, policy does not communicate, nor depends on, nor affects AAF container initialization in any way.

The only thing that may be confusing is the AAF title in the review, and the scope of this review is just the inclusion of a static root cert generated by AAF offline, and being placed in our policy store.   This may be needed locally within policy to honor server certificates from other components when processing calls.

In my set up, policy comes up OK.  I don't have AAF enabled, simply because is irrelevant for our proper working at present.

Based on this, I ask you to reconsider your -1.

Change has been successfully merged by Mike Elliott

Patch Set 2:

> Not a problem at all Mike!   I appreciate you taking the time to
 > test it.  I know you guys are super busy.

I hate to do this Jorge, but I have to revert this patch temporarily. It is not a problem with this patch per say. There is a size limit wrt how much external configuration can be "mapped" into Kubernetes in 1 helm deployment. In this case, when all components of ONAP are deployed together (I had a few turned off during my testing from a previous run) the limit is now exceeded. The right solution going forward is all components of ONAP should have their respective keystores, inside the containers. There needs to be mechanism that allows for each component to fetch/exchange certificates with AAF directly. This is instead of the need to externalize the entire keystore, to allow for configuration. Since that is a bigger issue than we can address right now, there is one of 3 things we can do. 

1.  leave the keystore inside your container for now, containing only the default keys (not configurable - short term work around)
2. leave the keystore inside your container and externalize the certificate that needs to be installed into the keystore - your container will need to have a mechanism to load the cert on start up
3. we have a strategy to reduce the amount of external config that should get us under the limit in Beijing - we will need a few days next week to implement and test this out
 
I will revert this patch for now to allow ONAP to be installed and continue testing. Let me know what you think of the options I have suggested or if you have any other recommendations.

Thanks in advance for your understanding,
Mike

Patch Set 2:

Thanks for the explanation Mike.   we'll go for option #2, we'll cook a generic truststore into the image, and then make delta changes on it from external configuration.
